coredata.dc:title: Novel oxidative cutting graphene oxide to graphene quantum dots for electrochemical sensing application

Zhang, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Here, for the first time, an integrated O3/H2O2/ultrasound process was proposed for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with uniform lateral dimension of 4–10nm. The hydroxyl radicals dominated cleavage effect was mainly responsible for tailoring graphene sheets into quantum dots. The resultant GQDs exhibited superior performance as electrode materials for electrochemically monitoring trace Cu2+ with a detection limit as low as 3×10−10 M.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Synthesis
Photoluminescence
Electrochemical sensors
Hydroxyl radicals
Cu2+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs-modified glass carbon electrodes
Electrochemical detection of Cu2+ with GQDs-modified GCEs
Microstructural and physical characterisations of GO and GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images: (a) GO, (b-d) GO after treated within different times (b) 0.5h, (c) 1h and (d) 3h, (e) statistical size distribution of GQDs for 100 particles for 180min, (f) HRTEM image of as-prepared GQDs, (g) electron diffraction pattern of as-prepared GQDs, (h–j) GO samples treated with (h) O3/H2O2, (i) O3/ultrasound and (j) H2O2/ultrasound.
(a) Raman spectra of GO and GQDs; (b) XPS spectra of GO; (c) XPS spectra of GO after 1h’s treatment and (d) XPS spectra of GO after 3h’s treatment.
(a) FTIR spectra of GO and as-prepared GQDs and (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of GO and as-prepared GQDs.

(a) ESR signals of DMPO spin trapping adducts produced in O3, H2O2 and O3/H2O2 processes; (b) PL spectra of GO products treated by various method for 1h with an excitation wavelength at 450nm; (c) PL spectra of GO samples treated by O3/H2O2/ultrasound process for 1h at different excitation wavelengths and (d) PL spectra of GQDs treated by O3/H2O2/ultrasound process for various hours with an excitation wavelength at 450nm.
Schematic illustration of the possible two-step generation mechanism of GQDs.
Electrochemical detection results of Pb2+ and Hg2+ (5mg/L).
Electrochemical detection of Cu2+: (a) voltammograms of GQDs-modified GCE at different Cu2+ concentrations and (b) calibration curve between current and concentration.
Summary of fitting results of XPS C1s peak split of GO, GO-1h (GO oxided by O3/H2O2/ultrasound process for 1h) and GQDs.

head.ce:title: Novel oxidative cutting graphene oxide to graphene quantum dots for electrochemical sensing application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Process and Engineering, Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Process and Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering (Tianjin)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Bio-based Materials
Qingdao Institute of Bioenergy and Bioprocess Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Jiawei Wen and Mingjie Li contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, for the first time, an integrated O3/H2O2/ultrasound process was proposed for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with uniform lateral dimension of 4–10nm. The hydroxyl radicals dominated cleavage effect was mainly responsible for tailoring graphene sheets into quantum dots. The resultant GQDs exhibited superior performance as electrode materials for electrochemically monitoring trace Cu2+ with a detection limit as low as 3×10−10 M.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), diminutive graphene sheets with lateral size less than one hundred nanometers, inherit most of the unique chemical and physical properties from grapheme as well as possess plenty of emerging properties due to quantum size effects [1–4]. GQDs show great potentials to be alternatives of conventional metal-based quantum dots (QDs), which are frequently limited by the cumbersome synthesis process, high cost, high toxicity and biological incompatibility [5,6]. To achieve desirable geometric and chemical properties for different applications, enormous efforts have been devoted to developing scalable and controllable synthesis of GQDs [7–15].

To date, the reported GQDs synthesis can be typically classified into bottom-up and top-down routes. The bottom-up strategies of unit cell assembly exhibit advantages on the tuning of size, composition and physical properties of the final GQDs by adjusting the organic precursors and reaction conditions. However, besides complicated procedures, GQDs fabricated via bottom-up approaches are usually multi-layered and exhibit inferior properties with respect to graphene. Top-down approaches, referring to cutting bulk graphene-based materials into micro-sized pieces, have drawn more concern to deliver and carry forward the superior properties from graphene.

The typical “top-down” cutting strategies include hydrothermal cutting, solvothermal cutting, microwave-assisted cutting and ultrasonic shearing (Table S1). These methods are responsible for large-scale solution-processable production from low-cost carbon sources. Nevertheless, they still suffer from the difficulty of thoroughly removing excess amount of oxidizing agent (e.g. HNO3 or Fe2+), which affect the purity of final products. In addition, a common problem often encountered is the environment-unfriendly (or even toxic) post-treatment. In all, the development of an effective and scalable top-down route for preparing high-quality GQDs still remains an urgent challenge.

Among the diverse applications of GQDs in the previous studies [16–20], quantitative and qualitative sensing on the basis of photoluminescence (PL) mechanism has attracted much attention due to GQDs’ unique physicochemical structure-dependent PL properties [21,22]. Similar to graphene, GQDs can also be used as a novel electrode material due to their electrochemical properties [23,24]. Nowadays, little effort was spared to exploit the potential applications of GQDs to detect trace heavy metal ions. It has been reported that GQDs were used as a facile and direct fluorescence sensor for the detection of Cu2+ with a detection limit of 10−8 M [25], but there is no publication about the electrochemical performances for the heavy metal ions utilizing GQDs.

We herein report an integrated O3/H2O2/ultrasound method for the synthesis of high-purity GQDs used to detect the trace heavy metal ions (e.g. Cu2+). The proposed one-step method is based on the hydroxyl radicals cleavage effect. It is an effective and green method without any post-treatment. The resultant GQDs exhibited excellent PL performances with uniform lateral dimensions of 4–10nm, and turned out to be suitable electrode materials to electrochemically monitor the trace of Cu2+ with a detection limit as low as 3×10−10 M.

GO was generated with a modified Hummers method as described in our previous work [26]. Afterwards, 20mL of 0.125mgmL−1 GO aqueous suspension was mixed with 1mL H2O2 (30wt%) in a quartz tube, continuously purged with 5mgmin−1 of O3 for 3h under ultrasonic condition (150W).Then the nitrogen (99.99% purity) was bubbled into the resulting suspension for 15min to terminate the reaction. The solid GQDs can be obtained via vacuum freeze drying. For comparison, the experiments by O3/H2O2/ultrasound solely were also carried out at the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs-modified glass carbon electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The original electrodes were glass carbon electrodes (GCEs) with diameter of 4mm (Aida Hengsheng Technology Co, Ltd, Tianjin). Before modification, the GCEs were well polished in 0.5μm and 50nm aluminium oxide slurry, sonicated in ethanol for 5min, rinsed with ultrapure water, and dried in air. The as-prepared GO and GQDs suspensions (10μL each) were uniformly daubed on the surface of GCEs. Then the modified GCEs were dried in air for at least 12h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of Cu2+ with GQDs-modified GCEs
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical experiments were carried out on a modular electrochemical workstation (Autolab PGSTAT 302N, Metrohm China Co. Ltd.) using a typical three-electrode system. The working electrodes were modified or bare GCEs, the auxiliary electrode was a platinum slice (20×20×0.1mm), and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) served as the reference electrode. The Cu2+ solution was measured by differential pulse anodic tripping voltammetry (DPASV) in Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (pH=4.1). All the electrochemical measurements were at room temperature (25°C). The scan rate was 50mVs−1, and the scan range was 0.6–0.8V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructural and physical characterisations of GO and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were observed using TEM (JEM 2100, JEOL, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectroscopy was carried out by a Spectra GX spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA), while the PL spectra were detected on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4500, Hitachi, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was obtained on an electron spectrometer (ESCALab 250Xi, VG Scientific, Britain) using Al Kα radiation at the power of 300W. The Raman spectra were obtained on a LabRAM HR800 Raman spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) at 514nm excitation. The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of radicals spin trapped by 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline(DMPO) were detected at ambient temperature on a JEOL (JES-FA200) spectrometer.

TEM was adopted to monitor the morphology variation of GO during each synthetic procedure. As shown in Fig. 1 a, GO displayed transparent and wrinkled micro-sheets, indicating its ultrathin structure. When GO was treated by O3/H2O2/ultrasound process, its morphology changed dramatically even within a short time period. Most of the GO micro-sheets could be disintegrated into nanometric fragments after 0.5h of reaction (Fig. 1b). As seen from Fig. 1c & d, the lateral size of the products decreased remarkably with the prolonging of reaction time. After treated for 3h, most of the obtained products showed a relatively uniform lateral distributions between 4 and 10nm (Fig. 1d & e). As expected, the HRTEM image (Fig. 1f) illustrated that the as-generated GQDs had 2D hexagonal symmetry crystal structure with an in-plane lattice constant of 2.46Å, exactly in accordance with that of grapheme [11]. The electron diffraction spectra showed perfect hexagon (Fig. 1g), confirming the successful synthesis of GQDs. Some porous nanosheets along with few fragments could be seen from GO samples treated by O3/H2O2 (Fig. 1h), O3/ultrasound (Fig. 1i) and H2O2/ultrasound (Fig. 1j). However, negligible change in the lateral size was observed compared to pristine GO when O3, H2O2 and ultrasound were not simultaneously adopted. This indicated the synergistic effect of O3, H2O2 and ultrasound in tailoring GO into quantum dots, and the three factors were indispensable.

GO and as-prepared GQDs were also tested by FTIR (Fig. 3 a). The absorption band at ∼1200cm−1 was typically assigned to epoxy and alkoxy (COC), which obviously decreased in GQDs. At the same time, carboxyl peak (–COOH, ∼1650cm−1) of GQDs was slightly increased compared with that of GO. The results showed that the resultant GQDs had abundant carboxyl. The UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 3b) showed two absorption peaks (230 and 310nm), which was ascribed to the π-π* transition of benzene (230nm) and n-π* transition of CO (310nm) [27]. More interestingly, the absorbance ratio of CC/CO decreased from 2.375 to 2.07, meaning more carboxyl generated after oxidation.

To further probe the internal mechanism, ESR was applied to detect the reactive species generated at different reaction conditions as shown in Fig. 4 a. Single addition of O3 or H2O2 could not generate OH, while the integration of H2O2 and ozone could efficiently produce OH, which could be verified by the obvious peaks assigned to OH.

It is well known that GQDs usually exhibit enhanced PL efficiency due to the quantum confinement effect compared to untreated GO [7,17]. As shown in Fig. 4b, PL intensities almost kept unchanged after the GO sample treated by O3/ultrasound or H2O2/ultrasound, while they moderately increased after GO was disposed by O3/H2O2. This was in accordance with the ESR result, suggesting the generated OH in O3/H2O2 posed a tailoring function during GQDs synthesis. More importantly, when ultrasound was introduced into O3/H2O2 system, PL property could be obviously improved, indicating a positive effect of ultrasound in promoting the oxidative tearing of GO into GQDs (Fig. 1d). The role of ultrasound was presumably to strengthen the molecular vibration, promote the OH generation and thus speed up the GO breakage into undersized fragments. Moreover, the PL spectra showed an obvious fluorescent emission at 520nm, which could attributed to the carboxyl abundance of as-prepared GQDs. The redshift along with the excitation wavelength from 400 to 460nm (Fig. 4c), was mainly responsible for the special PL property of GQDs which was not only affected by the size of GQDs but also their surface defects and surface chemistry [28,29]. As more GO fragments could be further cut into undersized pieces with the prolonging of O3/H2O2/ultrasound treating time, the PL intensities of the resultant sample turned stronger (Fig. 4d), which was also in agreement with the TEM result. The PL intensity of as-generated GQDs was also pH-dependent. As pH increased from 3.65 to 10, a sharp decrease even disappearance for the PL intensity was observed (Fig. S4), suggesting that the abundant carboxyl was responsible for the PL property.

Based on the previous analyses and relevant studies in literatures [28–30], a possible mechanism was proposed in Fig. 5 . The reaction process was mainly consisted of two steps. At the first stage (within the first hour), the CC/CC bands near the defects and sp3 carbons in GO were more prone to be oxidized and cut down by the generated OH assisted by ultrasound. The ultrasound was supposed to increase the amount of OH and also strengthen the molecular vibration, thus speeding up the GO breakage into undersized fragments (Fig. 1a–c). Meanwhile, the oxidation of OH led to an obvious increase of total oxygen-containing groups upon the GO surface (Fig. 2 and Table 1). With the prolonging of reaction time, the generated OH radicals accumulated and caused further cleavage and oxidization of the GO fragments under the promotion of ultrasound, leading to the generation of GQDs (Fig. 1d–g). During this stage, the surface CO and CO groups were oxidized into COOH (Fig. 2b–d), and some marginal ones were directly converted into CO2 and H2O (Fig. 2c–d). The GQDs with abundant COOH were hence generated.

In this study, a facile and efficient synthesis route of high-purity COOH-enriched GQDs was put forward, and it is based on the synergetic oxidative tailoring of GO into quantum dots by O3/H2O2/ultrasound process. The obtained GQDs with uniform lateral dimensions of 4–10nm exhibited potent PL properties as well as the excellent performances in electrochemically detecting. Moreover, it is expected to advance the fundamental research of quantum dots and the applications of as-generated GQDs in other potential fields such as solar cell, fluorescent detection, cellular imaging, and so on.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos.51102238, 51378487and 21307134), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project (2016M590670) and National High Technology Research and Development Program of China (863 Program) (No.2014AA06A513).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.mtcomm.2016.07.006.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
